headline: Sabah Bajau Laut Stateless Population Reaches 34,000
context: UNHCR estimates 34,000 Bajau Laut — sea nomads indigenous to the Sulu and Celebes Seas — live as stateless persons in Sabah, denied citizenship by both Malaysia and the Philippines.

cards.big: 34,000 Bajau Laut live in Sabah without citizenship in any country.
cards.sub: They predate national borders — their ancestral waters span 3 modern nations — yet no nation claims them.

cards.big: Bajau Laut children in Sabah cannot attend public schools.
cards.sub: Without birth certificates, they are invisible to the education system.

cards.big: Malaysia is not a signatory to the 1954 Convention on Statelessness.
cards.sub: Without treaty obligations, there is no legal framework compelling resolution.

cards.big: Bajau Laut traditional knowledge of marine ecosystems is globally unique.
cards.sub: UNESCO's 2024 report identifies their ecological knowledge as irreplaceable for coral reef management.

cards.big: A people older than the borders that exclude them. Statelessness is not their condition — it is what borders did to them.
Statelessness in the 21st century is a choice — made by states, not by stateless people. Malaysia can ratify the 1954 Convention or create a domestic pathway. Doing nothing is also a choice.
